---
title: "Day 44 — nanochat, part 1: tokenizer, base training, base eval"
subtitle: "Saturday, 5 September 2026 · Week 7 · Pipeline, RAG, evaluation · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 43](day-43.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 45 →](day-45.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
Every piece you built in Weeks 1-4 exists somewhere in this repo, wired to the piece before and after it - today you find each one and see what a production ordering of them looks like.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- You can list the stages in runs/speedrun.sh in order and, for each, name the script it runs, what it writes to disk, and roughly what share of the ~1.5 h and ~$48 it consumes.
- For each of tok_train.py, base_train.py and base_eval.py you can point at the piece of your own Week 1-4 code that does the same job, and name one thing nanochat does differently and why.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>205 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Read runs/speedrun.sh top to bottom without opening any script it calls. Write the ordered stage list, and for each stage record the input artifact, the output artifact, and the wall-clock and cost from the README.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Read tok_train.py against your own BPE from Week 3. Note the vocab size, the special tokens and what each one is for, the corpus it trains on, and where the merge loop actually runs. Write one paragraph on what your minbpe-style implementation would have to change to be usable here.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [55 min]{.task-min} Read base_train.py. Find the model definition, the parameter groups and which optimizer is applied to which group, the data loader, and the token budget - then check that budget against the Chinchilla ratio you worked through in Week 6 and say whether it is compute-optimal or deliberately not.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [30 min]{.task-min} Read base_eval.py. Identify the metric it reports and how it is normalized, and decide whether it is comparable to the perplexity you computed on your own GPT in Week 4. If it is not, write down why not.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [40 min]{.task-min} Read the launch writeup (discussion #1) and 'Beating GPT-2 for <<$100' (discussion #481), and note every place the author says a stage is cheap, expensive, or the bottleneck.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [nanochat](https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat) [repo]{.res-kind}
- [nanochat launch writeup (discussion #1)](https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat/discussions/1) [discussion]{.res-kind}
- [Beating GPT-2 for <<$100 (discussion #481)](https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat/discussions/481) [discussion]{.res-kind}
- [minbpe - your Week 3 reference](https://github.com/karpathy/minbpe) [repo]{.res-kind}
- [nanoGPT - your Week 4 reference](https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT) [repo]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
A one-page stage table: stage, script, input, output, time, cost, and the Week 1-4 day where you built the same thing yourself.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
Without reopening the repo: what does base_train.py produce, what consumes it next, and what breaks downstream if you change the tokenizer vocab size after base training has finished?
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 45, Sunday, 6 September 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Open chat_sft.py, chat_rl.py, chat_eval.py, chat_cli.py and infer_bench.py so tomorrow is reading, not file-hunting.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Skim the nanochat miniseries thread (discussion #420) for the mid-training explanation.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Decide tonight whether you are renting a GPU node tomorrow - if yes, create the account and check quota now; if no, drop it and stop thinking about it.
:::
:::
